properties.name: The Gate
properties.description: A worn stone archway marks the edge of the market. The stones are smooth from centuries of hands brushing them for luck on the way in — a tradition that predates any living vendor and which nobody has ever verified actually works. Beyond the arch, the noise hits you. The cobblestones underfoot are uneven, patched and repatched with whatever was handy, which in at least one spot appears to have been a flattened cooking pot. A faded sign bolted to the archway reads TINKERMARKET in letters made from dozens of different metals.
properties.secret: The sign letters are each made from a different dimensional metal. One letter — the K — is made from a metal nobody at the market can identify anymore. A void glass shard vibrates strongly near it. Tiv has been trying to buy the K for years.

properties.name: Market Square, South End
properties.description: The quieter end of the square, where the crowd thins near the gate. A wooden notice board bristles with pinned papers — requests, offers, warnings in scripts you can't read. A stone bench sits against a low wall, its surface polished by years of use. Nearby, a vendor tends a brass cart of roasted nuts that smell like cinnamon. The square opens up to the north.
properties.secret: The nut vendor's cart runs on a heat source from the same dimension as the forge fuel. The sharper smell is a spice that mildly enhances perception for about ten minutes after eating — if a player buys and eats the nuts, descriptions of materials they examine should include subtle hints about the material's properties. Regulars know this and snack here before browsing.

properties.name: Market Square
properties.description: A broad open area where several paths converge between stalls. Awnings of mismatched fabric stretch overhead, filtering the light into patches of color. Vendors line the edges — some behind wooden counters, others squatting beside blankets spread with goods. Foot traffic flows in every direction. Someone is trying to deliver a crate to a stall that has moved since yesterday. Paths lead off to the east, west, north, and northeast.
properties.secret: The square's layout shifts slowly over time — stalls migrate, paths widen or narrow. Long-time vendors claim the market rearranges itself to optimize foot traffic, though nobody can agree on whether this is literally true or just what happens when nobody's in charge.

properties.name: Rawstock Row
properties.description: A narrow lane between stalls piled high with raw materials. Bins of powders, coils of wire, jars of liquids in colors that shouldn't exist and in some cases are making a fairly convincing argument that they don't. The vendors here are specialists — they know what they're selling even if you don't. Stalls press in close on both sides, leaving just enough room to walk single file when two people try to pass, which they are constantly doing.
properties.secret: The electric smell is dimensional bleed — so many materials from different dimensions packed together in a small space creates a faint charge. Experienced vendors use this as a quality indicator: if the charge is strong, they've got good stock.

properties.name: Tiv's Materials
properties.description: A cramped stall halfway down Rawstock Row, every surface crammed with containers. Bins of dust and powder line wooden shelves. Wire coils hang from hooks overhead. Sealed jars sit in rows, their contents shifting when you're not looking directly at them. Behind the counter, Tiv sorts through a crate with quick, translucent fingers, holding things up to the light before filing them into bins with practiced efficiency.
properties.secret: Tiv organizes materials not by type but by dimensional compatibility — things that combine well are stored near each other. If a player asks Tiv about what goes with what, or examines which bins are next to each other, Tiv can hint at combinations. The jars that shift when you're not looking contain materials from a dimension with observer-dependent physics — staring at one long enough makes it hold still, and looking away lets it change.

properties.name: The Open Forge
properties.description: A communal workshop under a heavy canvas awning, open on three sides to the market. Workbenches are arranged in a rough semicircle around a central forge that glows with steady heat. Tools hang from racks on the support posts — hammers, tongs, clamps, things you don't have names for. The stone floor is scorched and stained. Grist moves between the benches, checking on work in progress and keeping the forge fed. A hand-painted sign reads: USE THE TOOLS. CLEAN UP AFTER. NO FIGHTING.
properties.secret: The forge's dimensional fuel keeps the entire workshop at a stable temperature that's ideal for working most cross-dimensional materials. Crafting done here is subtly more reliable than crafting done elsewhere in the market — materials bind more cleanly, edges seal better. Grist knows this and doesn't advertise it.

properties.name: Oddments Alley
properties.description: A side passage off the square, narrower and quieter than the main market. The stalls here are smaller, their wares stranger — things that don't fit neatly into categories. A vendor displays what might be musical instruments or surgical tools. Another sells jars of light in different colors. The noise from the main square fades quickly here. Things cost differently.
properties.secret: Oddments Alley attracts vendors selling things from dimensions that have less overlap with the market's usual trading partners. The goods here are harder to categorize because the dimensional physics they come from are more foreign. This is where the Fair Exchange begins — the transition from money to other forms of payment.

properties.name: The Fair Exchange
properties.description: An open court beyond Oddments Alley, paved in stone that's been swept clean. The stalls here are more permanent — wooden frames with cloth walls, some with actual doors. A sign at the entrance reads ALL PRICES POSTED in letters that look like they mean it. The vendors sit behind their counters with the patient attention of people who know exactly what they want and are prepared to wait for it.
properties.secret: The prices are posted, but not in money. One stall's sign reads: "One hour of your undivided attention." Another: "A favor, to be named." The signs are binding — not magically, but by the market's custom, which here amounts to the same thing. Vendors who don't honor posted terms lose their pitch.

properties.name: The Broker's Nook
properties.description: A small stall at the back of the Fair Exchange, walled on three sides by shelves of ledgers. Madame Wick sits behind a desk, writing in one ledger while consulting two others. A brass scale sits on the desk beside her, though what it weighs isn't obvious — one side holds a feather, the other side holds nothing, and they balance perfectly.
properties.secret: Madame Wick brokers exchanges between parties who can't agree on terms. The scale measures equivalence — not weight, but value as perceived by both parties. When both sides of a deal feel fair, the scale balances. She charges a commission: she keeps one detail from each transaction, which she records in her ledgers. She knows more about the market's dealings than anyone.

properties.name: The Rendering Yards
properties.description: The market opens up and gets louder. Canvas awnings give way to corrugated metal roofing. The ground underfoot changes from cobblestone to scored iron plate. Somewhere ahead, something large is being hit repeatedly with something larger. A hand-lettered sign bolted to a post reads RENDERING YARDS with an arrow. Below it, someone has added WATCH YOUR STEP, which given the state of the floor is less advice than understatement.
properties.secret: The iron plates on the ground are dimensional scrap — failed castings and offcuts from the workshops ahead. They've been trampled flat by foot traffic over years. Some still carry faint properties from their source dimensions: one plate near the entrance is always slightly warm, another hums if you stand on it.

properties.name: The Smelting Bay
properties.description: A wide, open workspace dominated by three crucibles set into a brick platform. Each one glows a different color — orange, blue-white, and something that isn't a color so much as a headache. Racks of molds line the walls. A four-armed worker pulls a crucible from the heat, pours a thin stream of molten something into a mold, replaces the crucible, and picks up a sandwich, all without pausing. The air tastes like hot pennies.
properties.secret: The three crucibles run at different dimensional temperatures — not just hot and hotter, but hot in different ways. Some materials only melt in the blue-white crucible. Others need the third one, which operates at a temperature that doesn't map to normal physics. The four-armed worker, Kelp, has been here long enough to know which crucible takes what by the sound a material makes when you tap it.

properties.name: The Nut Cart
properties.description: A brass cart on iron wheels, tended by a vendor with too many elbows. The cart's surface is a griddle heated from below by something that glows without flame. Nuts of various sizes roast in neat rows, each type in its own lane. A hand-lettered menu board lists names you can't pronounce and prices you can.
properties.secret: The vendor, Pell, roasts nuts from seven different dimensions. The spice blend includes a perception-enhancing compound — eating the nuts sharpens your senses for about ten minutes. Pell knows this and considers it a selling point, though they don't advertise it. The cart itself has been in the same family for three generations, which in Pell's species is about forty years.

properties.name: Wire & Filament
properties.description: A stall draped in coils. Wire of every gauge and material hangs from hooks, loops from pegs, and pools in baskets. The vendor sits in the middle of it all like a spider, splicing two wires together with a tool that looks like pliers crossed with a tuning fork. A sign reads WIRE & FILAMENT — CUT TO LENGTH.
properties.secret: The vendor, Spool, can hear the resonant frequency of any wire by running a fingernail along it. They stock coppervine alongside conventional wire, and they know that coppervine spliced to certain other wires creates a conductor that carries vibration and electricity simultaneously. They don't mention this unless asked, because the combination is expensive.

properties.name: Food Row
properties.description: A stretch of the market given over to food stalls. Steam rises from griddles and pots. A vendor fries something flat and round on a stone heated from beneath. Another serves bowls of broth from a vat tall enough to stand in. The smells change every few steps. A bench along one wall is occupied by vendors on their breaks, eating quickly.
properties.secret: Food Row serves as the market's informal commons — vendors from different sections come here to eat and gossip. Conversations overheard here hint at what's selling, what's scarce, and who's been buying in bulk. The broth vendor, who has been here longer than anyone, knows every regular by name and order.

properties.name: The Lens Grinder's Bench
properties.description: A workbench under a stretched canvas awning, its surface covered in glass dust. Racks of lenses in various stages of completion line the back wall — some clear, some tinted, one that appears to show a different room when you look through it. The grinder works with slow, deliberate strokes, pausing between each to hold the lens up and squint through it.
properties.secret: The lens grinder, Vero, makes lenses from dimensional glass — not just void glass, but glass from several dimensions with different optical properties. A lens that combines two types of dimensional glass can reveal things invisible to the naked eye: material resonances, dimensional stress fractures, the faint glow of active crafting bonds. Vero doesn't sell these openly because the last time they did, someone used one to cheat at a materials auction.

properties.name: The Bindery
properties.description: A stall hung with sheets of pressed fiber in dozens of colors and textures. Some are paper, some are cloth, some are something else — one sheet ripples faintly as if breathing. The vendor sits cross-legged on a cushion behind a low table, stitching pages into a cover with thread that glows faintly at the needle.
properties.secret: The vendor, Quill, binds books and ledgers, but the real trade is in binding agreements. Write a deal on Quill's paper, in Quill's ink, and the words hold — not magically, but because Quill's materials come from a dimension where written language has mild structural properties. A contract written here is slightly harder to break than one written elsewhere. Quill charges in stories: tell them something true that happened to you, and they'll bind a page for you.

properties.name: The Press House
properties.description: A shed-sized machine dominates this workspace — a dimensional press that flattens raw material into sheets. The operator feeds chunks of something dark into the top and cranks a wheel the size of a dinner table. Sheets of material emerge from the bottom, thin enough to see through and warm to the touch. The floor is littered with offcuts and failed runs.
properties.secret: The press can flatten almost any dimensional material into sheet form, which changes its properties — bulk void glass is brittle, but pressed void glass is flexible and can be cut with scissors. The operator, Drum, will press your materials for a fee. Pressed materials combine differently than their bulk forms, opening up crafting possibilities that don't exist otherwise.
